item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved it. Making a theorical SCP that is a form of argument is hard, making so in a historical context is harder, and serving it with this rigorous tone is perfectly welcome given its framework. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I admit it is a bit hard for me to understand—as you can see I come here from Chinese branch, and a translation version is still a chanllenge for me at first. But after the reading, I really appreciate this article, for its recognition of those who have been forgotten and those who exchanged their fame and income into respect.

Please accept my tiny respect for Ms Germain and the author. This article gave me the reason to stay here(and proved that there is still something great here), in contrast with the disgusting and disordering situation in currently Chinese branch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this article, but would like to request the addition of the Mathematical tag. Given the subject matter, I feel it is appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. I initially thought that there should be more on the potential threat of this anomaly, maybe using test logs or something, but upon re-reading I think that the implications of Germain’s usage and the containment procedures are enough. I also liked the dry, textbook-like tone. I think it really helps to emphasise the anomaly’s nature as a logical fallacy and that in-universe this is a fairly old SCP. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love logic stuff and an anomalous logic rule? Awesome! "It's not fake news, it's just the way it is!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to second this comment here.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I ended up loving this one a lot.

As a counterpoint to the other criticism, I LOVED the dry technical explanation of how the proof works in the middle of the document. In my mind it gave it a sense of verisimilitude that made me forget I wasn't reading a piece of fiction. It took me back to reading a textbook for college, although I understand why many wouldn't like that.

In the end I think this could end up being a niche article, one that wont work for most, but for those weirdos (like me) for whom it did, it will really work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A logical proof of anything that the human mind can't recognize as faulty, even if they know it must be. That does really feel like what the first known meme/cognitohazard would be. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not going to lie, I was pretty on the fence about this one.

On one hand, I like Germain's letters, and the implications she uses some anomalous logic to alter the path of Napoleon's conquest. Additionally, the fact that multiple governments are rolling out their own diet coke versions of this anomaly is quite chilling.

On the other hand, that bone-dry logic lesson in the middle of the document just kills any and all sense of pacing you had going into the article, and cause to really limp towards the finish.

I'm going to err on the side of an upvote, since the parts I liked were quite good. However, I don't think this will take of in any appreciable fashion until you trim down the lesson to keep what is designed to be entertainment, well, entertaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there's too much going on at once. There's

  • the proof itself (I agree with other commenters that the explanation collapsible could be much shorter)
  • the story of Germaine deducing the existence of 4000 (which unfortunately, much of her notes is spent describing what you already summarized in the text)

this was later corroborated by experimentation on Germain's part, by sending purposefully incorrect proofs (usually of nonsensical statements) to several prominent mathematicians such as Joseph-Louis Lagrange and Adrien-Marie Legendre, under various pseudonyms. While a few of the replies received pointed out that there might be an error in her work, none of them pointed to the SCP-4000 proof as faulty, instead citing false assumptions or mentioning potential new theories to explain the unexpected results.

  • the story of her saving Gauss from Napoleon
  • and then the list of other organizations attempting to exploit 4000 (which comes and goes like *that*, too abruptly to have any impact).

It's great to see another math-y skip but i think for this one you might need to choose a central focus and give the overall piece a pillar to lean on.

-1 for now, but I do hope you revise this some more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. I think the "Notes on the SCP-4000 syllogism" can be shorter though, It's a very long paragraph that explains a very simple concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you want containment handled by researchers without the proper background, then there should be explanations, but they should also be tailored to those researchers. Comparing with other articles, some give explanations of technical details in layman's terms (because this is not a website geared towards people with science degrees), while others forgo them completely, leaving the reader to exercise their Google-fu.

In other words, this is a good idea, but whatever you do, it's the presentation that matters most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy, I was fearing it'd turn out like this. I know the collapsible is too dry, as pointed by you and scpcrnp below, and I really considered just removing it. In the end, I left it for the benefit (?) of people not acquainted with classical logic (mostly for the examples), so I guess it definitely comes out as a textbook (and not a very well-written one).

Maybe I should just move the most relevant part (which would be the last paragraph) to the description and remove the rest, move it to a separate supplement page or make it clear it can be skipped, as the bulk of the story itself is in Addendum 1, and the knowledge required is that "SCP-4xxx is an anomalously persuasive argumentation scheme" and nothing else. Would something on the lines of "Researchers acquainted with the basics of symbolic logic may skip this section" work?1… however, I am aware that the dryness detracts from the rest of the piece, so maybe just axing it entirely would be for the better.

But well, it is at +7 so I suppose I didn't entirely fail at what I set up to do, which is good (better than what I expected, even). This also gives me some extra time to think it over and see what can be changed for the SCP to be less… boring. Thank you for the honest critique, it has been quite helpful.

Footnotes
1. In-universe, part of the containment is handled by people without a background in mathematics/logic, e.g. the censorship required, so this section would probably be present

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted because doing so is the only application of my philosophy degree since getting it.

I think this is a well-written article and I appreciated the thoroughness and thought put into it. But I agree that it is just too dry, too technical. And I typically enjoy dry, technical articles, way more so than the average reader. I'd say you need to introduce an intriguing or frightening application of the anomaly; it seems rife for some nefarious use or, in the spirit of the contest, responsibility for some major fallacious turn of thought in history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is definitely not what I'm getting at. I'm talking about the dryness of the textbook-style explanation in the collapsible. An SCP doesn't need to be dangerous to be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do agree its quite boring, and it it isn't that dangerous, as its only people going against logic because of the law, so it would be very, very hard for a disaster to happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate to say it, but I already took a logic class back in college, and this is about the most boring SCP article I have ever read. ._. I gave up before getting to the end of the first collapsible.

item.title: Well, let's bite the bullet.
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to my first SCP, an attempt at a minimalist entry for the 4000 contest. Thank you for reading!

Thanks to Neptune in chat (I don't know if their username here is the same as in IRC) and user DrAtasi from the forums for taking the time to read and comment on the drafts for this.

I'd have loved to get a bit more feedback before posting, but alas, the deadline was close and I probably wouldn't have been able to post this tomorrow. If this crashes and burns, well, lesson learned, ha. Still, thanks for your time and any further feedback is appreciated for future fixes or reimplementations.

EDIT: I forgot to mention that the portrait of Sophie Germain used is a public domain image. Relevant link from Wikimedia Commons here and source here.
